revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:箱根権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}}}
[[File:Haiden of Hakone Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Hakone Shrine (formerly Hakone Gongen)'s Haiden]] [[File:Hatsuhana_doing_penance_under_the_Tonosawa_waterfall.jpg|alt=Hatsuhana, the wife of Katsugorou Iinuma, who is struck by the waterfall and prays to Hakone Gongen,|thumb]]
'''{{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Gongen|qq=}}''' is a deity of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} that emerged from the fusion of the mountain worship of {{ill|Mount Hakone|en|Mount Hakone|qid=Q1067417|lt=Mount Hakone|1=Mount Hakone}} and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}, and it enshrines {{ill|Manjushri|en|Manjushri|qid=Q471696|lt=Manjushri|1=Manjushri}}, {{ill|Maitreya|en|Maitreya|qid=Q193461|lt=Maitreya|1=Maitreya}}, and {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Guanyin|1=Avalokiteśvara}} as its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=honji buddhas|1=Honji suijaku}}.
'''{{ill|Hakone Daigongen|ja|箱根大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Daigongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根神社|1=Hakone Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q705310}}''', together with {{ill|Izusan Shrine|en|Izusan Shrine|qid=Q611460|lt=Izusan Shrine|1=Izusan Shrine}}, are also known as '''{{ill|Hakone Nisho Gongen|ja|箱根二所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Nisho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504589|1=Hakone Nisho Gongen}}''', and in combination with {{ill|Mishima Taisha|en|Mishima Taisha|qid=Q650772|lt=Mishima Taisha|1=Mishima Taisha}}, they are called '''{{ill|Hakone Sansho Gongen|ja|箱根三所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Sansho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根権現|1=Hakone Gongen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11603375}}'''.
== History == On {{ill|Mount Hakone|en|Mount Hakone|qid=Q1067417|lt=Mount Hakone|1=Mount Hakone}}, there has been a tradition of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain Worship|1=Mountain worship}} directed toward the sacred mountain {{ill|Mount Kami (Hakone)|en|Mount Kami (Hakone)|qid=Q10871928|lt=Sacred Mountain|1=Mount Kami (Hakone)}}, and ceremonies were held at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Hakone)|en|Mount Koma (Hakone)|qid=Q31454757|lt=Komagatake|1=Mount Koma (Hakone)}}, from which the sacred mountain could be admired as a hallowed site. In particular, it is said that during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kōshō|en|Emperor Kōshō|qid=Q312841|lt=Emperor Kōshō|1=Emperor Kōshō}}, a sacred divination was performed at Komagatake enshrining the sacred mountain as a deity, which greatly contributed to the flourishing of mountain worship<ref name="yuisyo">[https://hakonejinja.or.jp/hakone/#link_01 Hakone Shrine: History]</ref>.
In the first year of {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|qid=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō Hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} ({{ill|AD 757|en|AD 757|qid=Q31135|lt=757 AD|1=AD 757}}), under imperial command, '''{{ill|Mankai|ja|万巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankai|1=Mangan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353818}}''' ascended Mount Hakone with the purpose of unifying its mountain worship, and after training for three years on the sacred mountain and at Komagatake, he attained the three aspects of the Gongen—the Hōtai, Zokutai, and Nyotai. The Hōtai is regarded as the manifestation of {{ill|Manjushri|en|Manjushri|qid=Q471696|lt=Manjushri|1=Manjushri}}, the Zokutai as the manifestation of {{ill|Maitreya|en|Maitreya|qid=Q193461|lt=Maitreya|1=Maitreya}}, and the Nyotai as the manifestation of {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Guanyin|1=Avalokiteśvara}}<ref>{{ill|Zonkaku|ja|存覚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zonkaku|1=Zonkaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6607009}}『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/821710/1/78 諸神本懐集]』 “The great deities of the Two Sites and Three Islands are none other than the Hakone Three Gongen. The Hōtai is Manjushri, the Mother of the Three Realms; the Zokutai is Maitreya, the Compassionate Teacher; and the Nyotai is the Fearless One, Guanyin.”</ref>.
According to tradition, Mankai, following a divine oracle, not only established the shrine enshrining {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Gongen|qq=}} (the present-day {{ill|Hakone Shrine|en|Hakone Shrine|qid=Q705310|lt=Hakone Shrine|1=Hakone Shrine}}) but also subdued the {{ill|Nine-Headed Dragon|ja|九頭竜伝承#箱根の九頭龍伝承と祭祀|lt=Nine-Headed Dragon|lt_ja=九頭龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nine-Headed Dragon|1=Kuzuryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13577962}} that had plagued the local people, and established a shrine dedicated to the dragon ({{ill|Nine-Headed Dragon Shrine|ja|九頭龍神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nine-Headed Dragon Shrine|1=kuzuryuujinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q108778735}}). Subsequently, in the course of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Hakone Gongen Shrine founded by Mankai and the {{ill|Tōfuku-ji at Mount Hakone|ja|箱根山東福寺|lt=Tōfuku-ji|lt_ja=東福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōfuku-ji at Mount Hakone|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504594|1=Tōfuku-ji Temple (Hakone)}} (later known as Kongō-in) became integrated, and devotion to Hakone Gongen flourished under the influence of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|東密|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhism|1=Tōmitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11526640}}, with many {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} practitioners ascending Mount Hakone, making it a prominent ascetic site in the Kantō region.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, once the Hakone Road was opened, travelers increasingly prayed to {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Gongen|qq=}} for safe passage<ref name="yuisyo" />.
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Gongen|qq=}} received profound veneration from {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|qid=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} and, following {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|qid=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}}, became a focus of faith for the Kantō samurai. Together with {{ill|Izusan Gongen|ja|伊豆山権現|1=Izusan Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izusan Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Izusan Gongen|qq=}}, they were known as the “Two Gongen,” giving rise to the custom of dual shrine visits<ref name="yuisyo" />.
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the 37th generation {{ill|Shōjitsu|ja|證実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōjitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and his elder brother {{ill|Ōmori Yoriharu|ja|大森頼春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Yoriharu|1=Ōmori Yoriharu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11436435}}, who belonged to the influential {{ill|Ōmori clan|ja|大森氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori clan|1=Ōmori clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11436378}} of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|qid=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} and {{ill|Suntō District, Shizuoka|en|Suntō District, Shizuoka|qid=Q1142259|lt=Sundō District|1=Suntō District, Shizuoka}}, sheltered the exiled {{ill|Kantō kubō|en|Kantō kubō|qid=Q860777|lt=Kamakura Governor|1=Kantō kubō}} {{ill|Ashikaga Mochiuji|en|Ashikaga Mochiuji|qid=Q4805073|lt=Ashikaga Mochi|1=Ashikaga Mochiuji}} during the {{ill|Uesugi Zenhō Rebellion|ja|上杉禅秀の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Zenhō Rebellion|1=Uesugi Zenshū's Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11359227}}, thereby rescuing him from peril. As a result, both the Ōmori clan and Hakone Gongen were granted vast estates on either side of Mount Hakone (in {{ill|Ashigarashimo District, Kanagawa|en|Ashigarashimo District, Kanagawa|qid=Q1193172|lt=Ashigara District|1=Ashigarashimo District, Kanagawa}} and {{ill|Suntō District, Shizuoka|en|Suntō District, Shizuoka|qid=Q1142259|lt=Sundō District|1=Suntō District, Shizuoka}}).
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117415390}}, although the Ōmori clan was eventually destroyed by the rising figure {{ill|Hōjō Sōun|en|Hōjō Sōun|qid=Q924929|lt=Hojo Soun|1=Hōjō Sōun}}, Soun himself valued control over Mount Hakone so highly that he sent his own child to serve as the head priest (betto) of Hakone Gongen; this child later became the 40th generation, Nagatsuna (subsequently known as {{ill|Hōjō Genan|en|Hōjō Genan|qid=Q10903136|lt=Hojo Genan|1=Hōjō Genan}})<ref>Ichimiya Sugiyama, “Kamakura Gongen Betto and the Military Power of the Muromachi Period”, in ''Policy in Eastern Provinces of the Muromachi Shogunate'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2014) ISBN 978-4-7842-1739-7 (original article in ''Kamakura'', No. 99, 2004)</ref>.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, with the establishment of a checkpoint in Hakone and the development of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|qid=Q1196306|lt=Tokaido|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}, Hakone Gongen—located at a vital transportation hub—received even more fervent devotion<ref name="yuisyo" />.
=== From the Meiji Period Onwards === Due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} edict of the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}} and the ensuing {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|qid=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}, the shrine originally devoted to Hakone Gongen based on Shugendō was renamed {{ill|Hakone Shrine|en|Hakone Shrine|qid=Q705310|lt=Hakone Shrine|1=Hakone Shrine}}<ref name="yuisyo" />. The head temple, {{ill|Tōfuku-ji at Mount Hakone|ja|箱根山東福寺|lt=Tōfuku-ji|lt_ja=東福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōfuku-ji at Mount Hakone|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504594|1=Tōfuku-ji Temple (Hakone)}}, was driven to closure (it is presumed to have been located near the parking area within the grounds of Hakone Shrine<ref name="koto"/>).
In the 6th year of the Meiji era (1873), {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} and {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|qid=Q269520|lt=Empress Dowager Shōken|1=Empress Shōken}} visited, and thereafter visits by members of the imperial family continued. In the 55th year of the {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Showa period|1=Hirohito}}, Emperor Shōwa and the Empress visited, and in the 56th year of Showa (1981), Crown Prince Kō-no-miya (the current {{ill|Reigning Emperor|en|Reigning Emperor|qid=Q8195432|lt=Emperor Naruhito|1=Reigning Emperor}}, Reiwa) also paid his respects<ref name="yuisyo" />.
== Notes == {{Notes Help}} {{reflist|25em}}
== Related Topics == *{{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo#Izu Exiles|ja|源頼朝#伊豆の流人|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo#Izu Exiles|lt_ja=源頼朝#伊豆の流人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo#Izu Exiles|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|箱根権現|version=101516308|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:箱根権現]]
